title: Arabic participles in Senhaja (Zerqet): TAM, agreement, and aktionsart-driven readings
introduction: This sketch describes how Arabic participial/adjectival forms are integrated into Senhaja (Zerqet) and how their interpretation is governed by lexical aspect (aktionsart). In Zerqet, Arabic-derived participles function as simple predicates (especially stative/resultative) and as post-nominal modifiers. Present-time predicative statives typically appear without a copula; explicit time reference (past/future) is supplied by independent TAM elements or by context. Morphologically, these borrowings preserve Arabic agreement in gender/number (Ø.M.SG, -a.F.SG, -in.PL), and they can even coexist with native agreement patterns. Existential meaning is encoded by a special participle-like predicate kayen/kayna ‘there is/are’ (negated makayen), and a frozen participial form bayna ‘apparently’ serves as an evidential particle. Dialectal comparison shows that Zerqet allows greater alternation between participle-based constructions and native verbal TAM morphology than some other Senhaja varieties (e.g., Ketama).

sections.description:
- Arabic participles are morphologically borrowed and adapted into Senhaja. Productive Arabic templates are retained, including active patterns (e.g., CaCC such as gales ‘sitting; idle’) and passive/resultative patterns (e.g., mCCuC such as meġsul ‘washed’). Suppletive borrowing is common (the participle may be Arabic-derived while the lexical verb is native, or vice versa), and some native Senhaja roots may even take Arabic participial patterns (pattern borrowing).
- Agreement: Borrowed participles/adjectives mark Arabic-style agreement: masculine singular (no suffix), feminine -a, plural -in (or -en). This agreement appears both in attributive position and, in Zerqet, also on predicative adjectives with plural subjects.
- Distribution: Arabic participial/adjectival forms function (i) attributively inside the NP (post-nominal), and (ii) predicatively as copula-less stative/resultative predicates in the present. They can also be complements inside copular clauses where the predicate is nominal.
Examples:
- Senhaja: l'albun n tṣawer qḏim — English: an old photo album — Explanation: Post-nominal modifier; **qḏim** is a masculine singular Arabic adjective used attributively.
- Senhaja: u lmeddraṣa qḏima dba gaɛ u teqqim. — English: That was an old school that doesn’t exist anymore. — Explanation: Feminine agreement on the attributive; **qḏima** shows the -a feminine.
- Senhaja: … kši lfakiha ġriḇa … — English: … some strange fruit … — Explanation: Attributive adjective; **ġriḇa** (feminine) agrees with a feminine head noun ‘fruit’.
- Senhaja: Ih, ḥetta nʷkna mezyanin ayneǧǧa. — English: Oh, we are fine too. — Explanation: Predicative plural agreement; **mezyanin** bears the plural -in with a plural subject.
- Senhaja: U daba iǧǧa-ṯen ssawalen s un lluġa mḫellṭa. — English: And now, he left them speaking a mixed language. — Explanation: Inside the NP, **mḫellṭa** ‘mixed’ shows feminine -a agreeing with **lluġa** ‘language’.

sections.focus: Predicative statives/resultatives in the present (no copula)
sections.description:
Zerqet freely uses Arabic participial/adjectival stems as bare predicates encoding current states (stative or resultative). No present-tense copula is used; the aspectual class of the predicate (stative/resultative) is what yields a ‘present state’ reading by default.
Examples:
- Senhaja: ah, sḫun! — English: Oh yes, you’re hot! — Explanation: Bare predicate; **sḫun** ‘hot’ is interpreted as a current state without a copula.
- Senhaja: Nek mezyan u ḵennami? — English: I’m fine, and you? — Explanation: Stative predicate **mezyan** ‘good/fine’ follows the subject with no copula.
- Senhaja: tḇanayed mmriḍ. — English: You look sick. — Explanation: Predicate **mmriḍ** ‘sick’ encodes a temporary health state.
- Senhaja: meḥtaža ḏiyes ḥit ġuri ṛṛyaḓiyaṭ nhariya! — English: But I need it for my math class today! — Explanation: The active-participial adjective **meḥtaža** ‘in need’ functions as the main predicate ‘I am in need (of it)’.

Borrowed Arabic adjectives/participles are post-nominal within the NP and agree in gender/number with the head noun. Their lexical aspect is still visible: many are inherently stative/resultative descriptors when used attributively.
Examples:
- Senhaja: l'albun n tṣawer qḏim — English: an old photo album — Explanation: Post-nominal **qḏim** ‘old’ modifies the noun phrase.
- Senhaja: u lmeddraṣa qḏima dba gaɛ u teqqim. — English: That was an old school that doesn’t exist anymore. — Explanation: **qḏima** agrees with ‘school’ (feminine).
- Senhaja: … kši lfakiha ġriḇa … — English: … some strange fruit … — Explanation: **ġriḇa** ‘strange’ (feminine) modifies ‘fruit’.
- Senhaja: U daba iǧǧa-ṯen ssawalen s un lluġa mḫellṭa. — English: And now, he left them speaking a mixed language. — Explanation: **mḫellṭa** ‘mixed’ (feminine) inside the NP.

sections.focus: Tense anchoring around participial predicates (present default; past/future by TAM markers)
sections.description:
The participial/adjectival predicate itself is morphologically stable. Time reference is supplied by independent TAM markers or by context. Present is the default with stative/resultative stems; future and past states are licensed by separate particles.
- Future state: a future particle (ša) scopes over the stative/resultative predicate.
- Past state: a past marker (iža ‘was’) locates the state in the past.
Examples:
- Senhaja: L'ustaḏ š aḏili mɛeṣṣeḇ matta uġuriš ddefṭar inu n ttamarim ikiḏi. — English: The teacher will be quite angry if I don’t have my notebook. — Explanation: **ša** marks future; the predicate **mɛeṣṣeḇ** ‘angry’ is a stative/resultative read in the future.
- Senhaja: iža ṯeḥḥlu. — English: They were sweet. — Explanation: **iža** anchors the adjectival predicate **(t)eḥḥlu** ‘sweet’ in the past.
- Senhaja: iža kayna gažiġ n iẓerf ǧǧiha lġerḇ ḥit lbuḥayra. — English: It was located down the road, towards the lake. — Explanation: **iža** + **kayna** ‘there was/it was’ expresses past existence/location of a state.

sections.focus: Existential/locative predicate kayen/kayna and its negation
sections.description:
Existence and location are encoded with the participle-like predicate kayen (M.SG) / kayna (F.SG), with negation makayen. These pattern as stative predicates and can be time-located with the past marker iža.
Examples:
- Senhaja: Waš kayen ši l'iḥtifal idduwar/ilvillaž, niġ ši ḥaža? — English: Is there a celebration in the village? — Explanation: Present existential with **kayen** ‘there is’.
- Senhaja: La makayen ḥetta ši l'iḥtifal. — English: No, there’s no celebration. — Explanation: Negated existential **makayen** ‘there isn’t’.
- Senhaja: iža kayna gažiġ n iẓerf ǧǧiha lġerḇ ḥit lbuḥayra. — English: It was located down the road, towards the lake. — Explanation: Past existential/locative **iža kayna** ‘there was / it was’.

The frozen participial form bayna functions as an evidential particle introducing inferences or apparent conclusions.
Examples:
- Senhaja: Bayna zi buḵra u netta iteɛɛyar ziyes. — English: It looks like he’s been playing with it all morning. — Explanation: **bayna** ‘apparently/it seems’ introduces an inference.
- Senhaja: Ṯamġarṯ yya bayna ḏ mayeḵ ɛweḏ, Ṯusi arḇa baš nnes. — English: This woman is surely your mother again, carrying a child on her back. — Explanation: **bayna** marks the speaker’s evidential stance (‘surely/apparently’).

sections.focus: Aktionsart and alternations: stative/resultative vs. progressive; Zerqet–Ketama variation
sections.description:
Lexical aspect crucially shapes readings. Arabic participles commonly encode states (including result states from prior events). With dynamic predicates, Zerqet allows broad alternation between participle-based progressives and native verbal TAM forms; Ketama relies more on participles and shows less interchangeability.
- Stative/resultative: participles present states without necessarily committing to a prior process (contrast with perfective-resultatives, which entail completion of a process).
- Progressive/durative: with dynamic predicates, both participle and imperfective can yield similar ‘ongoing’ readings in Zerqet.
- Prospective/future: participles like mašši ‘going’ can take a prospective value (≈ ‘be going to’), and in the documents are reported to grammaticalize as future markers in combination with a non-real element.
Dialectal note (documents): Zerqet permits alternation between participle and perfective/imperfective options where Ketama does not (e.g., the perfective alternative to a participle is ungrammatical in Ketama but available in Zerqet).
Examples (Zerqet-oriented):
- Senhaja: nukna gals-in ḏa zgi (i)ḏeǧǧi — English: We have been sitting here since yesterday. — Explanation: Active participle **gals-in** ‘sitting’ (plural -in) encodes an ongoing/stative situation anchored by a since-phrase.
- Senhaja: nukna n-eskurem ḏa zgi (i)ḏeǧǧi — English: We have been sitting here since yesterday. — Explanation: Native perfective can yield a comparable ‘since’-anchored state in Zerqet; contrasts with Ketama, where this alternative is excluded per the documents.
- Senhaja: telt iyyam w nukna maššy-en — English: We have walked/have been walking for three days. — Explanation: Active participle **maššy-en** ‘walking’ expresses a prolonged dynamic eventuality.
- Senhaja: telt iyyam w nukna n-ɛeddu — English: We have walked/have been walking for three days. — Explanation: Imperfective **n-ɛeddu** alternates with the participle for a similar progressive/durative reading in Zerqet.
- Senhaja: netta mašši azekka — English: He is going (~He will go) tomorrow. — Explanation: Active participle **mašši** ‘going’ has prospective value in a future-time frame; documents report further grammaticalization with a non-real marker (not exemplified here to respect example limits).

sections.focus: Resultatives and entailments (mCCuC-type) vs. processual perfectives
sections.description: Arabic passive/resultative participles (e.g., mCCuC-type) encode states such as meġsul ‘washed’ or meḥlul ‘open’, which present the resultant condition without necessarily asserting the preceding process in the clause. In contrast, a perfective-resultative verb form implies a process culminating in a state. In Zerqet usage, this contributes to the preference for participles in state descriptions (including many borrowed lexicalized adjectives) and for native verbal morphology when the dynamic event itself is foregrounded. (No additional examples provided here beyond those above, as the contrast is primarily interpretive and established by the documents.)
